coredata.dc:title: A pH-induced charge convertible nanocomposite as novel targeted phototherapy agent and gene carrier
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Zheng
Hou, Bo
Yang, Ying
Jiang, Lanxuan
Xie, Anjian
Shen, Yuhua
Zhu, Manzhou
coredata.dc:description: Photo therapy for cancer has attracted massive attentions due to the advantages of excellent selectivity, non-invasion and low toxicity. However, the application of traditional photo agents in cancer therapy is limited by the defects of single function, poor water solubility, and weak targeting to tumors. Herein, a tumor extracellular microenvironment responsive and photo-sensitive nanocomposite based on the combination of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs), histidine, and hemin was developed, possessing high-performance phototherapy for cancer. The histidine grafted nanocomposites could undergo intriguing charge conversion from negative-charged surface to positive one in mildly acidic tumor extracellular microenvironment (pH ∼ 6.5), leading high affinity to negatively charged cancer cell membrane, which resulted in enhanced targeting ability of the nanocomposites. Meanwhile, the photothermal property of N-CDs and photodynamic ability of hemin were simultaneously enhanced via the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect in CC NCs, which endued the synthesized nanocomposites with excellent bi-phototherapy for cancer cells. Moreover, the as-prepared nanocomposites could deliver plasmid into tumor cells for effective gene transfection, which possessed potential application in gene therapy. The present work demonstrates that the obtained nanocomposites could achieve multimodal cancer therapies and provides the inspiration for the design and synthesis of other multifunctional materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Charge convertible nanocomposites
pH sensitivity
Phototherapy
Gene carrier
Multimodal cancer therapies

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure of CC NCs
In vitro transfection of CC NCs/DNA condensates

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Environment-Friendly Polymer Materials
National Nature Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Aerospace Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 21631001 , 21571002 , and 21671001 ) and Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Environment-Friendly Polymer Materials .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and simulated response of multifunctional CC NCs.
TEM images of (A) N-CDs, (B) HN-CDs, and (C) CC NCs, respectively; (D) Zeta potentials of CC NCs in different simulated physiological environments (PBS, pure medium, and serum medium).

(A) UV–Vis spectra of (a) Histidine, (b) N-CDs, (c) HN-CDs, (d) Hemin, and (e) CC NCs, respectively (the inset presents enlarged visible spectrum of obtained CC NCs at the wavelength of 550–690 nm); (B) FT-IR spectra of (a) N-CDs, (b) HN-CDs, and (c) CC NCs, respectively; (C) Raman spectra of (a) N-CDs, (b) HN-CDs, and (c) CC NCs; (D) XRD pattern of CC NCs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of (a) N-CDs, (b) up-conversion of N-CDs, and (c) CC NCs; (B) Zeta potentials of N-CDs, HN-CDs, and CC NCs at the pH values of 5.5, 6.5, and 7.4, respectively; (C) the photothermal curves of water and the dispersion of N-CDs, HN-CDs, and CC NCs with different concentration; (D) the photodynamic effect of CC NCs.

Bright-field and fluorescence microscopy images of HepG2 cells incubated with (a, f) Control groups (without samples), (b, g) N-CDs, (c, h) HN-CDs, (d, i) pure hemin, and (e, j) CC NCs, (a–e) without laser irradiation and (f–j) with laser irradiation (638 nm, 1 W·cm−2), respectively. All groups were treated with DCFH-DA (10 μmol·L−1) at 37 °C for 50 min before irradiation.

(a) In vitro phototherapy effects of N-CDs, HN-CDs, and CC NCs with various concentrations against HepG2 cells with or without laser irradiation, (b) In vitro phototherapy effects of CC NCs (100 μg·mL−1) against HepG2 cells at different pH values of 6.5 and 7.4 with or without laser irradiation.
Fluorescence microscopy images of (a) FITC-dextran and FITC-dextran/CC NCs complexes co-incubated with HepG2 cells at pH of (b) 7.4 and (c) 6.5.

Fluorescence microscopy images of HepG2 cells incubated with (a, e) Control (without samples), (b, f) N-CDs, (c, g) HN-CDs, and (d, h) CC NCs, (a–d) without laser irradiation and (e–h) with laser irradiation (638 nm, 1 W·cm−2); HepG2 cell were dyed in blue by Hoechst 33,342 (1), red by PI (2) and the merged images of both above (3), respectively.

(a) Gel electrophoresis of CC NCs/DNA and PEI/DNA condensates at different mass ratios respectively; (b) pH-triggered release of DNA from CC NCs/DNA condensates at mass ratio of 5:1; (c) Time-dependent size distributions of CC NCs by DLS at pH 5.5, 6.5, and 7.4, respectively.
Fluorescence images for GFP transfections of PEI/DNA and CC NCs/DNA condensates with different mass ratios at pH 7.4 and 6.5, respectively.
Transfection efficiencies of PEI/DNA and CC NCs/DNA condensates with different mass ratios at pH 7.4 and 6.5 determined by the flow cytometry.

head.ce:title: A pH-induced charge convertible nanocomposite as novel targeted phototherapy agent and gene carrier

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Collaborative Innovation Center of Modern Bio-manufacture, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Collaborative Innovation Center of Modern Bio-manufacture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photo therapy for cancer has attracted massive attentions due to the advantages of excellent selectivity, non-invasion and low toxicity. However, the application of traditional photo agents in cancer therapy is limited by the defects of single function, poor water solubility, and weak targeting to tumors. Herein, a tumor extracellular microenvironment responsive and photo-sensitive nanocomposite based on the combination of nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs), histidine, and hemin was developed, possessing high-performance phototherapy for cancer. The histidine grafted nanocomposites could undergo intriguing charge conversion from negative-charged surface to positive one in mildly acidic tumor extracellular microenvironment (pH ∼ 6.5), leading high affinity to negatively charged cancer cell membrane, which resulted in enhanced targeting ability of the nanocomposites. Meanwhile, the photothermal property of N-CDs and photodynamic ability of hemin were simultaneously enhanced via the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect in CC NCs, which endued the synthesized nanocomposites with excellent bi-phototherapy for cancer cells. Moreover, the as-prepared nanocomposites could deliver plasmid into tumor cells for effective gene transfection, which possessed potential application in gene therapy. The present work demonstrates that the obtained nanocomposites could achieve multimodal cancer therapies and provides the inspiration for the design and synthesis of other multifunctional materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, the rising incidence of cancer engenders huge threat to human health, impeding the development of society [1]. Despite the theranostics of cancer expanding rapidly in the past decades, how to enhance the precision and efficacy of cancer therapy is still a giant challenge to scientists [2–5]. Photo-therapy, including photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT), due to the advantages of excellent selectivity, non-invasion and low toxicity, is becoming an efficient technology for clinical cancer therapy [6]. However, traditional PT and PS such as porphyrin [6], phthalocyanine [7], methylthionine chloride [8], indocyanine green [9], grapheme [10], fullerene [11], titanium dioxides [12], and gold nanomaterials [13] widely applied in cancer therapy, possess the problems of single function, poor water solubility, easy aggregation, as well as weak targeting to tumors, hindering the application of phototherapy in clinic. Although phototherapy is an effective avenue for cancer treatment, the ability for eradication of tumor is finite. In the past two decades, gene therapy as one of the most potential methods, which may cure cancer absolutely, attracts enormous attention of researchers [14]. Up to now, many carriers such as polycations (including polyamine, polysaccharides, and polypeptides), cationic lipids, and cyclodextrin are developed to improve the intracellular delivery and enhance the transfection of gene in tumor cells [15–17]. However, the low stability, inefficient transfection, and aggregating proneness of these materials are still limiting the application of gene therapy in medical field.

Due to the swift development of nanotechnology, compositing various functional materials to obtain multipurpose nanocomposite is an impactful avenue for the improvement of cancer therapy [18–20]. N-CDs, a kind of nanomaterial composited by nitrogen, oxygen and carbon mainly, possess excellent water solubility, containing massive functional groups, for instance, carboxyl and amido, which are advantageous to combine with other groups [21,22]. Meanwhile, N-CDs can up-convert the absorbed visible light and near infrared (NIR) light to emit blue fluorescence, increasing the light availability of nanomaterials. The integrin-targeting N-CDs with up-conversion ability nanocomposites were developed and used as photothermal therapy agent [8]. However, the author did not investigate the synergistic photodaynamic therapy to achieve bi-phototherapy by FRET effect. As we known, traditional nanomaterials without specific targeting for tumor cells can not cure cancer selectively and availably. In order to enhance the targeting ability of nanomaterials, a novel technology based on the charge interaction among nanomaterials and cell membrane was proposed [23]. Zhao’s group prepared the charge-convertible carbon dots to deliver drug for imaging-guided cancer therapy [24]. But, the nanosystem was used for photothermal or photodynamic or synergistic cancer therapy without consideration. Our group reported hemin could absorb UV and Vis light at the range of 220–500 nm, producing cytotoxic 1O2 under the irradiation to kill cancer cells rapidly [25]. Kennedy reported that large amounts of hemin could be accumulated locally to form aggregates at tumor site which could induce apoptosis, because the abnormal metabolism of cancer cells resulted in subacid micro-environment [26]. Yet the water insolubility of hemin is the key barrier for its application in cancer therapy.

To solve the mentioned problems in cancer therapy, we designed and synthesized a multifunctional nanocomposite using N-CDs as photothermal agent, hemin as photodynamic agent, and histidine as targeting agent by amidation among the three components. The scheme of the synthesis and simulated response of multifunctional charge-convertible nanocomposites (CC NCs) was shown in Fig. 1 . The synthesized nanocomposites possessed FRET-enhanced photothermal and photodynamic properties simultaneously, achieving bi-phototherapy for cancer cells. Furthermore, the charge-convertible ability endowed the as-prepared nanocomposites with intelligent cell targeting effect, which could cure cancer effectively. Moreover, the obtained CC NCs could act as efficient gene carrier, facilitating the gene transfection in cancer cells availably. And more, the appropriate size of the provided CC NCs owned enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, which could be accumulated at tumor site by passive targeting efficacy. But until now, the work like ours that the photothermal, photodynamic and gene therapies as well as the dual targeting are integrated in a simple N-CD-based biological nanomaterial has not been reported. Thus, this study paves a new way for the design and synthesis of novel multifunctional materials applied in the drug carries, medicine and other areas.

Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (C2H6OS), urea (CO(NH2)2), and hydrochloric acid (36.0–38.0 wt%), were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China). Citric acid, sodium hydroxide, l-histidine hydrochloride, hemin (C34H32ClN4O4Fe), 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) (C20H14O), and phosphate buffer solution (PBS) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. LLC. Hoechst 33342, propidium iodide (PI), penicillin, streptomycin, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), pancreatin solution (25 wt%), high glucose medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Sangon Biotech Inc. Dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), 1-ethyl-3-[3–dimethylaminopropyl] carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), and N-hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Macklin Inc. The dialysis bags (3.5 kDa) were obtained from Biosharp Co., Ltd (P. R. China). 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) buffer, tris(hydroxymethyl)methyl aminomethane-acetic acid-ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (TAE) buffer, agarose, loading buffer, and Goldview were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. All the agents were analytical pure and used as received without further purification. The water used in the experiments was deionized (Milipore Mili-Q grade) with resistivity of 18.0 MΩ·cm.

UV–Vis spectra were obtained from a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer, and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected using a NEXUS-870 FTIR spectrometer with the KBr pellet technique. Raman spectra were conducted by a Via-Reflex laser confocal Raman spectroscopy. Crystallinity of samples were studied using a DX-2700 X-ray diffraction (XRD) (CuKα radiation, λ = 1.54056 Å, 40 V, 100 mA, 6 °/min) to collect the power X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. Morphological details of the obtained samples were recorded by Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (SU 1510, 10 kV) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM 100SX, 200 kV). The photothermal effects of pure water, N-CDs, HN-CDs, and CC NCs composites were studied using a Fluke Ti32 infrared thermography camera to obtain the thermographies. The size distributions and zeta potentials of all samples were collected by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and laser doppler electrophoretic methods of Malvern zetasizer nano (UK).

Briefly, citric acid (1.0 g) and urea (0.7 g) were dissolved in 30 mL of pure water. The mixed solution was sonicated for 30 min and then sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (50 mL per autoclave). Subsequently, the autoclave was heated to 150 °C for 5 h, followed by cooling down to room temperature. The obtained navy-blue dispersion was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (3.5 kDa) over night. Finally, the dialyzed suspension was frozen in ultra-low temperature freezer (−86 °C) for 10 min and then was subjected to freeze-drying to obtain the black-green solid powders, i.e., N-CDs.

150 mg of N-CDs was dispersed in 30 mL of pure water. Then 300 mg of EDC and 300 mg of NHS were added to above dispersion with sonication for 30 min followed by the addition of l-histidine (60 mg) and stirred for 24 h. The mixed dispersion was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (3.5 kDa) over night. Finally, the dialyzed dispersion was freeze-dried to obtain the black-green HN-CDs and stored at 4 °C.

1 mL of HN-CDs aqueous dispersion (5 mg·mL−1) was dissolved in 20 mL of DMSO followed by the addition of different mass of hemin (0.01, 0.05, and 0.25 g, respectively). After stirring for 30 min, the mixed dispersion was sealed in Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (50 mL per autoclave) and reacted at 160 °C for 6 h, followed by cooling down to room temperature. The dark-black dispersion was firstly dialyzed in NaOH solution (0.01 mol·L−1) through a dialysis membrane (3.5 kDa) for 24 h to remove unreacted hemin and DMSO then dialyzed in deionized water by a dialysis bag (3.5 kDa) for 24 h. The final dispersion was freeze-drying to obtain dark-black powders of CC NCs.

The as-prepared N-CDs, HN-CDs, and CC NCs were dispersed in DMEM medium with different concentrations of 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg·mL−1, respectively. The human hepatocellular carcinoma cell line (HepG2) suspended in DMEM medium was seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 103 each well (200 μL) and incubated for 24 h. After washing each well by PBS for 3 times, the cells were incubated with only 100 μL of DMEM medium (control), and with 100 μL of DMEM medium containing the samples of N-CDs, HN-CDs, and CC NCs (0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg·mL−1, respectively) for 24 h, respectively. After washing each well by PBS for 3 times, 20 μL of MTT and 100 μL of DMEM medium were added to each well with sequential incubation for 4 h. As contrast, the irradiated groups were taken out with the irradiation of 638 nm laser (1 W·cm2) for 10 min and then incubated for 24 h. 100 μL of DMSO solution was then added for dissolving the insoluble formazan crystals. The optical density of samples was measured using an Elisa reader at 490 nm to calculate the cell viability by formula (1).
(1)
Cell viability
=
O
D exp
- O
D bla
O
D con
- O
D bla
× 100 %
where ODbla, ODexp, and ODcon are the optical densities of blank, experimental and control groups respectively.

HepG2 cells were seeded on culture dishes (35 mm, provided by atTek Corporation) for 24 h to allow the cells to attach to the surface of the wells. After treatment with the DMEM suspension containing N-CDs, HN-CDs, and CC NCs (0.1 mg·mL−1) respectively under 6 h incubation, the cells were washed three times with PBS. The incubated group without samples was used as the control. Then all groups were cultured with DCFH-DA (10 μmol·L−1) at 37 °C for 50 min. Cells were further washed three times with PBS before the illumination groups were irradiated by laser light (638 nm, 1 W·cm2) for 3 min. Intracellular ROS was detected by means of DCFH which could be oxidized to a highly fluorescent derivative, 2,7-dichloro-fluorescein (DCF) in the presence of hydroxyl (OH) radicals. Attempts to detect 1O2 were made by using the same method.

To quantify the cell uptake, HepG2 cells (5 × 104 per well) were cultured in a 6-well plates at 37 °C for 48 h in DMEM. After that, the cells were washed twice by serum-free medium, incubating with serum-free medium for 1 h. Then, CC NCs were added to the fresh serum-free medium containing FITC-dextran (1 mg·mL−1), and the pH value of the mixture was adjusted to 7.4 or 6.5, following by sonication for 20 min to obtain the FITC-dextran/CC NCs complexes [27]. After that the as-prepared complexes-containing medium were incubated with cells at 37 °C for 1 h. Washing cells by PBS buffer for three times, the fluorescence microscope images were obtained by an excitation wavelength of 488 nm.

For further investigating the cytotoxicity of CC NCs, Hoechst 33,342 and PI were used to assess the relative cell viability between treated and untreated HepG2 cells. Briefly, HepG2 cells were seeded into 6-well plates at a density of 5 × 104 each well and maintained in an incubator at 37 °C, 5% CO2 for 24 h. After that 3 mL of DMEM suspension containing CC NCs (0.2 mg·mL−1) was added to each well, followed by incubating in dark for 24 h. When the incubation time lasted for 4 h, the irradiated groups were taken out and irradiated using 638 nm laser (1 W·cm2) for 10 min. After further 24 h incubation, PBS buffer (pH 7.4) was used to replace the medium, then 0.5 mL of Hoechst 33,342 (10 μg·mL−1) and 0.5 mL of PI (10 μg·mL−1) were added to each well for 15 min. The final cells were washed twice with PBS and observed by an inverted fluorescence microscopy to obtain fluorescent images.

Plasmid DNA pEGFP was dissolved in 20 mM HEPES (pH 7.4) with a concentration of 0.1 mg·mL−1. Various condensates of CC NCs/DNA mass ratios were obtained by adding DNA solution (50 mL) to CC NCs dispersion (50 mL) in 20 mM HEPES (pH 7.4) with different CC NCs concentrations, vortexing for 30 s and incubating at room temperature for 20 min. The CC NCs/DNA condensates were examined by gel electrophoresis. 10 mL of each condensate was mixed with 2 mL loading buffer and then loaded onto a 1% agarose gel containing 0.2 mg·mL−1 of Goldview, and electrophoresed at 100 V for 30 min in TAE buffer. Images of gels displaying DNA migration were obtained by GelDoc XR (Bio-Rad, USA).

To investigate the stability of condensates, CC NCs/DNA condensates with mass ratio of 5:1 were dispersed into PBS buffer (pH 7.4, 6.5, and 5.5) at room temperature. The released DNA from CC NCs/DNA condensates was examined by gel electrophoresis at the time point of 0, 12, 24, and 48 h. The fluorescence intensity of naked DNA bands was look upon as 100% free DNA. The rate of DNA release was calculated by densitometry basing on electrophoresis patterns.

To confirm the synthesized CC NCs are novel gene carriers, the plasmid of enhanced green fluorescence protein (pEGFP) was used as the model DNA molecule to evaluate expression of the internalized DNA. HepG2 cells were transfected by CC NCs/DNA condensates with different mass ratios of 5:1, 10:1, and 20:1 at pH of 7.4 and 6.5, respectively. PEI/DNA condensates at the mass ratio of 1:1 were used as control. After incubating 48 h, the cells were washed with PBS and observed under the fluorescence microscope to examine the expression of the intra cellular EGFP. Moreover, to obtain the accurate transfection efficiencies of all groups, a FACSCalibur 501 flow cytometer (Beckman Coulter) was used to score the percentage of green fluorescence emitting cells. Additionally, GFP positive gate was set basing on condensates which contained firefly luciferase avoiding autofluorescence of cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of CC NCs
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize the morphology of N-CDs, HN-CDs, and CC NCs, TEM images were obtained (Fig. 2 ). The N-CDs (Fig. 2A) and HN-CDs (Fig. 2B) were both nanodots with the diameter of ca 3 nm, proving that the decoration of histidine for N-CDs was non-influence to the size of the HN-CDs. From Fig. 2C, the obtained CC NCs possessed spherical morphology with the uniform size about 100–150 nm. Compared with N-CDs and HN-CDs, the enlarged size of CC NCs may be due to the self-assembly of hemin grafted amphiphilic composites during the dialysis. In order to investigate the stability of as-prepared CC NCs, zeta potentials of synthesized CC NCs in different simulated physiological environments (PBS, pure medium, and 10% serum-containing medium, respectively) were collected (Fig. 2D). According to the previous report, the absolute value of zeta potential which was larger than 20 mV illustrated the good stability of the nanoparticle in dispersion.[28] The obtained average zeta potentials of the CC NCs were about −32.7, −35.6, −35.7 and −44.9 mV respectively, indicating the synthesized CC NCs possessing excellent stability in simulated physiological environments. Thus, the aforementioned evidences indicated that the synthesized CC NCs possessed the suitable size and good stability which were favorable for the application in cancer therapy.

UV–Vis spectra were further performed (Fig. 3 A) to investigate the optical properties of CC NCs. Curve c was related to HN-CDs possessing the absorption peak of histidine at 203 nm attributed to the imidazole group (Curve a) and N-CDs at 339 nm due to the existence of C = O (Curve b) simultaneously. The peak of histidine with red shift from 334 nm to 339 nm may be induced by the amidation between histidine and N-CDs. Curves d and e showed the spectra of hemin and the obtained CC NCs respectively. The free hemin in sodium hydroxide solution displayed a Soret peak at 388 nm and a weaker peak at 620 nm. After combination with HN-CDs, the Soret band of CC NCs (395 nm) had a red shift compared with that of monomeric hemin, which may be due to the amidation between the HN-CDs and the hemin [29]. The inset in the enlarged visible spectrum of CC NCs showed wide absorption peak at the range of 540–680 nm assigned to the grafted hemin confirming that the CC NCs could absorb the photons of laser with the emitting wavelength ranging from 600 to 650 effectively. Brancaleon et al considered that the penetrating ability of laser in 600–700 nm to most tissues is 50–200% more than laser in 400–500 nm, so the better effect of phototherapy could be achieved [30]. And Jaque’s group thought the emitting wavelength of therapeutic laser should be corresponding to the absorption band of the photosensitizer, which could induce the efficiency of photo conversion for phototherapy [13]. Therefore, the 638 nm laser was suitable for CC NCs to perform cancer phototherapy. Thus, the analysis above illustrated the successful combination of histidine, N-CDs, and hemin, and the potential application in cancer phototherapy.

Fig. 3B showed the FT-IR spectra of different samples. In Fig. 3B-a, the broad peak at 3420 cm−1 was attributed to NH stretching vibration and the strong peak at 1570 cm−1 was assigned to NH bending vibration, which proved the existence of amino group in N-CDs. The peaks at 3200, 1400, and 1200 cm−1 were corresponded to COH stretching vibration, OH bending vibration, and COH scissoring vibration, respectively. Moreover, the peaks at 1650 and 1128 cm−1 were attributed to CO stretching vibration and CNHC asymmetrical stretching vibration. These evidences confirmed the massive existence of amino groups and carboxyl groups in N-CDs [29]. According to Fig. 3B-b, the peaks of HN-CDs at 3200 and 1570 cm−1 presented declined intensities compared to those of N-CDs, which may be caused by amidation between histidine and N-CDs. Meanwhile, the enhanced peak of CO vibration at 1640 cm−1 indicated more content of CO in HN-CDs. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 3B-c, the new peaks at 2870, 2920, and 966 cm−1 were assigned to CH stretching vibration and characteristic signal of pyrrole structure, which were induced by grafted hemin in CC NCs [31,32]. All the above mentioned results demonstrated that the obtained CC NCs are composed with histidine, hemin, and N-CDs, possessing amounts of amine and amide groups.

Raman spectroscopy was used to confirm the qualitative of N-CDs, HN-CDs, and CC NCs, respectively (Fig. 3C). All samples exhibited two major features of the D band and the G band which were observed at round 1385 and 1580 cm−1, respectively [24]. The peak of CC NCs at 1385 cm−1 which was higher than those of N-CDs and HN-CDs demonstrated the content of disordered carbon was increased. The reason may due to that the grafted hemin induced the enhancement of edge defects of CC NCs. XRD was also used to characterize the synthesized CC NCs (Fig. 3D). The broad peak centered at around 25.5° also confirmed the amorphous structure of the CC NCs, which was corresponding to the results of Raman spectra.

In order to understand the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect between N-CDs and hemin in the CC NCs, the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 4 A-a) and CC NCs (Fig. 4A-c) were obtained respectively. Fig. 4A-b showed the up-converted fluorescence of N-CDs. Under the excitation at 330 nm, the N-CDs exhibited a strong fluorescence emission with the maximum wavelength at 440 nm. The up-convertible ability of N-CDs was confirmed by the fluorescence up-conversion spectrum excited at 638 nm with the same fluorescence emission peak at 440 nm. However, CC NCs showed invisible fluorescence emission under the excitation at 330 nm, demonstrating the occurrence of fluorescence quenching after the combination of N-CDs with hemin and histidine. From Fig. 3A-b and A-d, it is seen that N-CDs and hemin both possessed strong light absorption at the range of 400–450 nm, which could absorb the blue fluorescence with emission peak at ca 440 nm of N-CDs effectively. The FRET effect from N-CDs to N-CDs caused the self-quenching phenomenon in CC NCs, which enhanced the photothermal property of the synthesized CC NCs [33]. Meanwhile, the FRET effect from N-CDs to hemin also enhanced the photodynamic ability of hemin, inducing intensive phototherapy efficacy of the synthesized CC NCs [8]. Hence, the photothermal and photodynamic abilities of the obtained CC NCs were enhanced synchronously by FRET effect according to the smart design of the combination of N-CDs and hemin.

To investigate the charge convertible property of CC NCs, the zeta potentials of N-CDs, HN-CDs, and CC NCs were obtained at the different pH values of 5.5, 6.5, and 7.4, simulating the microenvironment of lysosomes, tumor extracellular and normal physiological condition, respectively (Fig. 4B). When pH value changed from 7.4 to 5.5, N-CDs exhibited non-charge convertible characteristic with the consistent negative zeta potentials [24]. However, zeta potentials of HN-CDs and CC NCs changed from negative value to positive one with the same transformation of pH value, demonstrating the variety of anionic surface to cationic one induced by the protonated imidazole groups of histidine at low pH condition. Zeta potentials of CC NCs were higher than those of HN-CDs at the same acidic conditions, demonstrating that CC NCs were more stable than HN-CDs at the same condition, which could be attributed to the more amounts of imidazole groups on the surface of CC NCs. Thus, the charge convertible property of CC NCs engendered the charge targeting ability by the electrostatic interaction between CC NCs and tumor cell membrane.

The photothermal effects of all samples were shown in Fig. 4C. Under continuous irradiation of 638 nm laser for 10 min, the temperature elevations of N-CDs and HN-CDs dispersion (1 mg·mL−1) both increased about 6 °C. However, the temperature increase of water was ignorable. As comparison, the irradiated CC NCs dispersions (0.1, 1, and 5 mg·mL−1) emerged remarkable temperature changes increasing about 26, 25, and 27 °C respectively, which were significantly higher than those of other samples. The above evidences indicated the synthesized CC NCs exhibiting excellent photothermal performance, which could be used as a novel photothermal agent for cancer therapy.

For further studying photodynamic effect of the CC NCs, DPBF was used as an indicator to detect the engendered 1O2 or ROS in the presence of CC NCs under the 638 nm laser irradiation (1 W·cm-2) for 10 min [34]. It is clearly seen that the absorption peak intensity of DPBF at 410 nm obviously declined with the increase of irradiation time (Fig. 4D) while that had unapparent changes in the dark within the same time, illustrating the obtained CC NCs were effective photodynamic agents, which have potential application in cancer therapy. The preceding results indicated that the prepared charge convertible CC NCs possessed outstanding photo-converting ability achieving simultaneous photothermal and photodynamic effects.

Herein, as shown in Fig. 5 , DCFH-DA was used to assess intracellular ROS production from the treated HepG2 cells by different samples (100 μg·mL−1, 6 h) [8]. Fig. 5a2-e2 exhibited no fluorescence in HepG2 cells co-incubated with PBS, N-CDs, HN-CDs, pure hemin, and CC NCs, indicating that all samples without laser irradiation could not generate ROS. Fig. 5f1-h1 and f2-h2 present the images of the HepG2 cells incubated with PBS, N-CDs, and HN-CDs under the laser irradiation, showing well growth of HepG2 cells and invisible DCF fluorescence, which confirm that only laser irradiation or with N-CDs or HN-CDs could not induce intracellular ROS effectively. Meanwhile, the slight fluorescence (Fig. 5i2) in HepG2 cells incubated with pure hemin after irradiation indicated pure hemin could not induce intracellular ROS efficiently. The origin may be due to that the insoluble hemin was hardly internalized by cells, which generated tiny amounts of ROS in cells under laser irradiation. However, the irradiated group of CC NCs exhibited the obvious DCF green fluorescence in Fig. 5j2, indicating the effective generation of ROS in HepG2 cells. All the results proved that CC NCs were high-performance photodynamic agents which could induce the production of intracellular ROS, benefitting phototherapy of cancer.

In order to study the phototherapy effects of N-CDs, HN-CDs, and CC NCs, the MTT assay is performed (Fig. 6 a). The N-CDs, HN-CDs, and CC NCs with various concentrations (0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg·mL−1) were incubated with HepG2 cells as non-irradiated groups and irradiated groups, respectively. The groups incubated without any samples were used as control groups. The cell viabilities of non-irradiated groups were all over 90%, revealing that there was no obvious cytotoxicity of all samples and also confirming CC NCs possessed good biocompatibility. The irradiated group (638 nm, 1 W·cm−2, 10 min) of control showed negligible cytotoxicity with the cell viabilities above 90%, indicating the laser irradiation could not influence the growth of cancer cells. The cell viabilities of the N-CDs and HN-CDs groups exhibited a few decrease after lase irradiation, proving that N-CDs and HN-CDs both had photothermal abilities to kill cancer cells. However, the irradiated group of CC NCs exhibited obvious cytotoxicity with the cell viabilities of 60%, 40%, 20%, and 10% at the concentrations of 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg·mL−1 respectively. The reason may be that the CC NCs not only possess the excellent photothermal efficacy, but also have photodynamic ability endowed by hemin, achieving synergetic photothermal and photodynamic therapies. To investigate the charge convertible ability of CC NCs for cancer therapy, in vitro phototherapies of CC NCs against HepG2 cells at different pH value of 6.5 and 7.4 with or without laser irradiation were performed (Fig. 6b) [23]. All non-irradiated groups showed the cell viabilities as high as about 85%, demonstrating the pH values had very small influence to the cell survival viability. Meanwhile, the irradiated groups without CC NCs also emerged high cell viabilities of over 80%, which revealed only the laser irradiation could not engender cytotoxicity. Nevertheless, both the irradiated groups with CC NCs at pH value of 6.5 and 7.4 showed obvious cytotoxicity, with the cell viabilities reductions of about 15% and 60%, respectively. The obtained results illustrated that the charge-conversion of CC NCs at pH 6.5 was advantageous to enhance the phototherapy effect for cancer therapy, which may be due to the positively charged CC NCs targeting to cell membrane is more easily than the negatively charged one. Thus, the prepared CC NCs possessed excellent phototherapy ability, with the property of charge-conversion triggered by tumor environment, performing outstanding selectivity for cancer therapy.

The uptake of CC NCs in HepG2 cells was studied by FITC-dextran and fluorescence microscopy. Cells in Fig. 7 a have no fluorescence demonstrating that FITC-dextran could not be uptaken by HepG2 cells. Comparing to the group co-incubated at pH 7.4 (Fig. 7b), the bright green fluorescence (Fig. 7c) indicated the more internalization of FITC-dextran/CC NCs complexes within cells for the group co-incubated at pH 6.5. The reason may be that CC NCs possessed better targeting ability at low pH condition than that at high pH condition, which caused internalization of large amounts of FITC-dextran/CC NCs complexes by HepG2 cells.

Base on the evidences shown in Fig. 8 , the non-irradiated groups of HepG2 cells incubated with control, N-CDs, HN-CDs, and CC NCs which stained by Hoechst 33,342 and PI, emitted blue fluorescence (a1-d1) and almost invisible red fluorescence (a2-d2) respectively, as well as blue fluorescence (a3-d3) in the merged images, indicating that all the cells grew well without obvious cell apoptosis. The irradiated group of control showed blue fluorescence (e1) and negligible red fluorescence (e2), demonstrating that the mere laser irradiation could only induce negligible apoptosis of HepG2 cells. The groups of N-CDs and HN-CDs also exhibited blue fluorescence (f1-g1) as well as dim red fluorescence (f2-g2), indicating that the photothermal effects of N-CDs and HN-CDs could not kill cancer cells effectively. However, when CC NCs co-incubated cells were irradiated by laser for a short time, an intense red fluorescence could be seen in the emerged image (h3), which showed that the phototherapy effect of CC NCs caused a large amount of cell apoptosis [34]. Therefore, the CC NCs are novel phototherapy agents harboring excellent synergetic therapy abilities of photothermal and photodynamic.

In order to study the gene delivery ability of as-obtained CC NCs, gel retardation assays (Fig. 9 a) were performed. The negligible fluorescence of CC NCs/DNA condensates at the mass ratios higher than 3:1 showed that CC NCs were able to load DNA effectively, indicating the obtained CC NCs were suitable gene carriers in current condition. Because the isoelectric point of histidine was at pH 7.59, the large amounts of imidazole groups in CC NCs possessed positive charge at pH 7.4 yet, which could bind with negative charged DNA forming CC NCs/DNA condensates. To further understand the gene release properties, CC NCs/DNA condensates were dispersed in HEPES buffer with different pH values to simulate the environments of physiological condition (7.4), tumor site (6.5), and lysosome (5.5) respectively. According to Fig. 9b, the released DNA at pH 5.5 was about 25.6%, 68.6%, and 94.1% at the time points of 12, 24, and 48 h respectively, illustrating CC NCs/DNA condensates possessed pH-triggered release ability of DNA. For comparison, the CC NCs/DNA condensates at pH of 6.5 and 7.4 showed invisible fluorescence during 0–48 h, which demonstrated CC NCs/DNA condensates kept stable in the simulated environments of physiological condition and tumor site. For exploring the pH sensitive release mechanism, the size distributions of CC NCs/DNA condensates were obtain at different time points (0, 12, 24, and 48 h) under pH condition of 7.4, 6.5, and 5.5 by DLS measurements respectively. From Fig. 9c, the average size (0 h) of CC NCs/DNA condensates at pH of 5.5 was about 190 nm which was similar to those (0 h) at 7.4 and 6.5. However, the average size (pH 5.5) of CC NCs/DNA condensates enlarged to ca 531, 615, and 955 nm at the time points of 12, 24, and 48 h, respectively. The reason was due to that hemin in CC NCs could easily form large aggregates by μ-oxo bridges at low pH condition [35,26]. Meanwhile, the average size (pH 7.4 and 6.5) of CC NCs/DNA condensates kept stable without obvious changes during 0–48 h. Thus, the pH-triggered release property may be induced by the size enlargement which destroyed the original stabilization of CC NCs/DNA condensates, causing the release of DNA in the time-dependent process. Moreover, invariable size distributions of CC NCs/DNA condensates under pH of 7.4 and 6.5 after 48 h also showed the good stabilities of CC NCs/DNA condensates, which were suitable gene carriers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro transfection of CC NCs/DNA condensates
ce:section.ce:para: For the investigation of gene delivery of CC NCs, GFP transgene expressions were performed in PEI/DNA and CC NCs/DNA condensates co-incubated HepG2 cells, respectively (Fig. 10 ) [36]. It is clearly seen that the numbers of cells emitting green fluorescence co-incubated CC NCs/DNA condensates at pH 6.5 were much more than those at pH 7.4, indicating the synthesized CC NCs were pH-sensitive gene carriers. Furthermore, the green fluorescence densities in CC NCs groups at pH 6.5 were similar to that in commercial PEI group, demonstrating that the obtained CC NCs could be suitable gene carrier with the potential gene therapy for cancer accordingly.

In summary, novel multifunctional nanocomposites combined with N-CDs, histidine, and hemin was synthesized successfully. The synthesized nanocomposites possessed pH-induced charge conversion property which could achieve charge targeting, greatly enhancing the effect of cancer treatment. Meanwhile, the as-synthesized nanocomposites performed excellent synergistic photothermal and photodynamic therapy, which could be used as a novel phototherapy agent for cancer therapy effectively. Furthermore, the prepared nanocomposites could deliver plasmid into tumor cell to realize effective gene transfection, holding huge potential application for genetic therapy. All above results indicate that the as-synthesized nanocomposites are multifunctional phototherapy agents with enhanced electrostatic targeting to tumor cells, possessing gene delivery ability, which could implement multimodal cancer therapies.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (No. 21631001, 21571002, and 21671001) and Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Environment-Friendly Polymer Materials.
